**Christ Church** ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Ædes Christi*, the temple or house, *[ædes](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/aedes "wikt:aedes")*, of Christ, and thus sometimes known as "The House") is a [constituent college](/wiki/Colleges_of_the_University_of_Oxford "Colleges of the University of Oxford") of the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") in [England](/wiki/England "England").[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4) Founded in 1546 by [King Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England"), the college is uniquely a joint foundation of the university and the cathedral of the Oxford diocese, [Christ Church Cathedral](/wiki/Christ_Church_Cathedral,_Oxford "Christ Church Cathedral, Oxford"), which also serves as the college [chapel](/wiki/Chapel "Chapel") and whose [dean](/wiki/Dean_of_Christ_Church,_Oxford "Dean of Christ Church, Oxford") is *ex officio* the college head.

As of 2022, the college had 661 students.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-4) Its grounds contain a number of architecturally significant buildings including [Tom Tower](/wiki/Tom_Tower "Tom Tower") (designed by [Sir Christopher Wren](/wiki/Christopher_Wren "Christopher Wren")), [Tom Quad](/wiki/Tom_Quad "Tom Quad") (the largest quadrangle in Oxford), and the Great Dining Hall, which was the seat of the [parliament](/wiki/Oxford_Parliament_(1644) "Oxford Parliament (1644)") assembled by [King Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") during the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"). The buildings have inspired replicas throughout the world in addition to being featured in films such as *[Harry Potter](/wiki/Harry_Potter_(film_series) "Harry Potter (film series)")* and *[The Golden Compass](/wiki/The_Golden_Compass_(film) "The Golden Compass (film)")*, helping Christ Church become the most popular Oxford college for tourists with almost half a million visitors annually*.[[5]](#cite_note-5)*

The college's alumni include 13 [British prime ministers](/wiki/List_of_Prime_Ministers_of_the_United_Kingdom_by_education "List of Prime Ministers of the United Kingdom by education") (the highest number of any Oxbridge college), as well as former prime ministers of [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") and [Ceylon.](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") Other notable alumni include [King Edward VII](/wiki/Edward_VII_of_the_United_Kingdom "Edward VII of the United Kingdom"), [King William II of the Netherlands](/wiki/King_William_II_of_the_Netherlands "King William II of the Netherlands"), [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn"), writers [Lewis Carroll](/wiki/Lewis_Carroll "Lewis Carroll") (author of *Alice in Wonderland*) and [W. H. Auden](/wiki/W._H._Auden "W. H. Auden"), philosopher [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke"), and scientist [Robert Hooke](/wiki/Robert_Hooke "Robert Hooke"). Two Nobel laureates, [Martin Ryle](/wiki/Martin_Ryle "Martin Ryle") and [John Gurdon](/wiki/John_Gurdon "John Gurdon"), studied at Christ Church.[[6]](#cite_note-6) [Albert Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein") is also associated with the college. The college has [several cities](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch") and places named after it.[[7]](#cite_note-:02-7)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christ_Church,_Oxford&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Hall_of_Christ_Church_College.jpg/250px-Hall_of_Christ_Church_College.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hall_of_Christ_Church_College.jpg)

Hall of Christ Church

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Oxford_Library_of_Christ_Church.jpg/250px-Oxford_Library_of_Christ_Church.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oxford_Library_of_Christ_Church.jpg)

Christ Church's library in the early 19th century

In 1525, at the height of his power, [Cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_(Catholicism) "Cardinal (Catholicism)") [Thomas Wolsey](/wiki/Thomas_Wolsey "Thomas Wolsey"), [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor") of England and [Archbishop of York](/wiki/Archbishop_of_York "Archbishop of York"), suppressed [St Frideswide's Priory](/wiki/St_Frideswide%27s_Priory "St Frideswide's Priory") in Oxford and founded Cardinal College on its lands, using funds from the dissolution of [Bayham Old Abbey](/wiki/Bayham_Old_Abbey "Bayham Old Abbey"), [Wallingford Priory](/wiki/Wallingford_Priory "Wallingford Priory") and [other minor priories](/wiki/Thomas_Wolsey#Church_reforms "Thomas Wolsey").[[8]](#cite_note-8) He planned the establishment on a magnificent scale, but fell from grace in 1529, with the buildings only three-quarters complete, as they were to remain for 140 years.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1531 the college was itself suppressed, but it was refounded in 1532 as King Henry VIII's College by [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England"), to whom Wolsey's property had [escheated](/wiki/Escheat "Escheat"). Then in 1546 the King, who had broken from the [Church of Rome](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") and acquired great wealth through the dissolution of the monasteries in England, refounded the college as Christ Church as part of the reorganisation of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), making the partially demolished priory church the cathedral of the recently created Diocese of Oxford.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Christ Church's [sister college](/wiki/List_of_Oxbridge_sister_colleges "List of Oxbridge sister colleges") in the University of Cambridge is [Trinity College, Cambridge](/wiki/Trinity_College,_Cambridge "Trinity College, Cambridge"), founded the same year by Henry VIII. Since the time of [Queen Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I_of_England "Elizabeth I of England") the college has also been associated with [Westminster School](/wiki/Westminster_School "Westminster School"). The dean remains to this day an *ex officio* member of the school's governing body.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Major additions have been made to the buildings through the centuries, and Wolsey's Great Quadrangle was crowned with the famous [gate-tower](/wiki/Tom_Tower "Tom Tower") designed by [Christopher Wren](/wiki/Christopher_Wren "Christopher Wren"). To this day, the bell in the tower, [Great Tom](/wiki/Great_Tom_(bell) "Great Tom (bell)"), is rung 101 times at 9 pm measured by [Oxford time](/wiki/Oxford_time "Oxford time"), meaning at 9:05 pm [GMT](/wiki/GMT "GMT")/[BST](/wiki/British_Summer_Time "British Summer Time") every night, once for each of the 100 original scholars of the college, plus one more stroke added in 1664. In former times this was done at midnight, signalling the close of all college gates throughout Oxford. Since it took 20 minutes to ring the 101, the Christ Church gates, unlike those of other colleges, did not close until 12:20 am. When the ringing was moved back to 9:00 pm, Christ Church gates still remained open until 12.20, 20 minutes later than any other college. Although the clock itself now shows GMT/BST, Christ Church still follows Oxford time in the timings of services in the cathedral.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[King Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") made the Deanery his palace and held his Parliament in the Great Hall during the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War").[[12]](#cite_note-12) In the evening of 29 May 1645, during the second [siege of Oxford](/wiki/Siege_of_Oxford "Siege of Oxford"), a "bullet of IX lb. weight" shot from the [Parliamentarians](/wiki/Roundhead "Roundhead")' warning-piece at [Marston](/wiki/Marston,_Oxfordshire "Marston, Oxfordshire") fell against the wall of the north side of the Hall.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Several of Christ Church's deans achieved high academic distinction, notably [Owen](/wiki/John_Owen_(theologian) "John Owen (theologian)") under the [Commonwealth](/wiki/English_Commonwealth "English Commonwealth"), [Aldrich](/wiki/Henry_Aldrich "Henry Aldrich") and [Fell](/wiki/John_Fell_(bishop) "John Fell (bishop)") in the [Restoration](/wiki/English_Restoration "English Restoration") period, [Jackson](/wiki/Cyril_Jackson_(priest) "Cyril Jackson (priest)") and [Gaisford](/wiki/Thomas_Gaisford "Thomas Gaisford") in the early 19th century and [Liddell](/wiki/Henry_Liddell "Henry Liddell") in the high Victorian era.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

For more than four centuries Christ Church admitted men only; the first female students at Christ Church matriculated in 1980.[[14]](#cite_note-:1-14)

## Organisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christ_Church,_Oxford&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Organisation")]

Christ Church, formally titled "The Dean and Chapter of the Cathedral Church of Christ in Oxford of the Foundation of King Henry the Eighth",[[1]](#cite_note-chch-ox-statutes-1) is the only academic institution in the world which is also a [cathedral](/wiki/Cathedral "Cathedral"), the seat ([cathedra](/wiki/Cathedra "Cathedra")) of the [Bishop of Oxford](/wiki/Bishop_of_Oxford "Bishop of Oxford"). The [visitor](/wiki/Visitor "Visitor") of Christ Church is the reigning [British sovereign](/wiki/British_sovereign "British sovereign")[[15]](#cite_note-:0-15) (currently [King Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III")), and the Bishop of Oxford is unique among English bishops in not being the visitor of his own cathedral.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The head of the college is the Dean of Christ Church.[[16]](#cite_note-christchurch1718-16) Christ Church is unique among Oxford colleges in that its head of house, who is head of both college and cathedral, must be an Anglican cleric appointed by the Crown as [dean](/wiki/Dean_(religion) "Dean (religion)") of the cathedral church. The dean lives on site in a grand 16th-century house in the main quadrangle. The college's activities are managed by a senior and a junior censor (formally titled the *Censor Moralis Philosophiae* and the *Censor Naturalis Philosophiae*) the former of whom is responsible for academic matters, the latter for undergraduate discipline. They are chosen from among the members of the governing body. A *censor theologiae* is also appointed to act as the dean's deputy.

The form "Christ Church College" is considered incorrect, in part because it ignores the cathedral, an integral part of the unique dual foundation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Governing body

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christ_Church,_Oxford&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Governing body")]

The governing body of Christ Church consists of the dean and [chapter](/wiki/Chapter_(religion) "Chapter (religion)") of the cathedral, together with the "Students of Christ Church", who are not junior members but rather the equivalent of the [fellows](/wiki/Oxbridge_Fellow#university_fellows_dons "Oxbridge Fellow") of the other [colleges](/wiki/Colleges_of_the_University_of_Oxford "Colleges of the University of Oxford"). Until the later 19th century, the students differed from fellows in that they had no governing powers in their own college, as those resided solely with the dean and chapter. The governing body of Christ Church now has around 60 members. Serving alongside the seven members of chapter, the other members include statutory professors and associate professors with joint appointments (employed both by the University and Christ Church) as well as early-career career development fellows on fixed-term contracts. Sir [John Bell](/wiki/John_Bell_(physician) "John Bell (physician)") and Sir [Tim Berners-Lee](/wiki/Tim_Berners-Lee "Tim Berners-Lee") are both members of the governing body of Christ Church.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Influences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christ_Church,_Oxford&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Influences")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Christ_Church_Cathedral_from_across_the_Meadow.jpg/250px-Christ_Church_Cathedral_from_across_the_Meadow.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christ_Church_Cathedral_from_across_the_Meadow.jpg)

Christ Church Cathedral from the east across [Christ Church Meadow](/wiki/Christ_Church_Meadow,_Oxford "Christ Church Meadow, Oxford")

The college buildings and grounds are the setting for parts of [Evelyn Waugh](/wiki/Evelyn_Waugh "Evelyn Waugh")'s *[Brideshead Revisited](/wiki/Brideshead_Revisited "Brideshead Revisited")*, as well as a small part of [Lewis Carroll](/wiki/Lewis_Carroll "Lewis Carroll")'s *[Alice's Adventures in Wonderland](/wiki/Alice%27s_Adventures_in_Wonderland "Alice's Adventures in Wonderland")*.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) More recently it has been used in the filming of the movies of [J. K. Rowling](/wiki/J._K._Rowling "J. K. Rowling")'s *[Harry Potter](/wiki/Harry_Potter "Harry Potter")* series and also the film adaptation of [Philip Pullman](/wiki/Philip_Pullman "Philip Pullman")'s novel *[Northern Lights](/wiki/Northern_Lights_(Pullman_novel) "Northern Lights (Pullman novel)")* (the film bearing the title of the American edition of the book, *[The Golden Compass](/wiki/The_Golden_Compass_(film) "The Golden Compass (film)")*). Distinctive features of the college's architecture have been used as models by a number of other academic institutions, including the [NUI Galway](/wiki/NUI_Galway "NUI Galway"), which reproduces [Tom Quad](/wiki/Tom_Quad "Tom Quad"). The [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago"), [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University"), and [Kneuterdijk Palace](/wiki/Kneuterdijk_Palace "Kneuterdijk Palace") have reproductions of Christ Church's dining hall (in the forms of [Hutchinson Hall](/wiki/Hutchinson_Hall,_University_of_Chicago "Hutchinson Hall, University of Chicago"), the dining hall of [Risley Residential College](/wiki/Risley_Residential_College "Risley Residential College"), and the Gothic hall of Kneuterdijk Palace,[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) respectively). [ChristChurch Cathedral](/wiki/ChristChurch_Cathedral,_Christchurch "ChristChurch Cathedral, Christchurch") in New Zealand, after which the [City of Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch") is named, is itself named after Christ Church, Oxford. Stained glass windows in the cathedral and other buildings are by the [Pre-Raphaelite](/wiki/Pre-Raphaelite "Pre-Raphaelite") [William Morris](/wiki/William_Morris "William Morris") group with designs by [Edward Burne-Jones](/wiki/Edward_Burne-Jones "Edward Burne-Jones").[[32]](#cite_note-CCglass-32)[[33]](#cite_note-UTex-33)

### Resident animals on grounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christ_Church,_Oxford&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Resident animals on grounds")]

Historically, there has been a resident tortoise for the annual Oxford tortoise races.[[34]](#cite_note-34) However, since 2020, due to the pandemic, there has not been a tortoise. Recently, there have been two "resident" ducks, which can be seen in [Tom Quad](/wiki/Tom_Quad "Tom Quad"), affectionately named "Tom" and "Peck" after two of the famous quadrangles in Christ Church.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The Mercury fountain also houses carp, notably a large koi carp named George, which was a gift from the Empress of Japan. A heron may also be frequently seen visiting the pond as their hunting ground. This stopped, in September 2022, when the fishes were moved to a spacious lake home somewhere in Oxfordshire while the College perform essential maintenance on the pond.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Outside the [Meadow Building](/wiki/The_Meadow_Building "The Meadow Building") in the Christ Church Meadow, there are also cows present during the day. The cows are of rare [English Longhorn](/wiki/English_Longhorn "English Longhorn") breed.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

## Cathedral choir

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christ_Church,_Oxford&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Cathedral choir")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Christ_Church_Cathedral_Interior_1%2C_Oxford%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg/250px-Christ_Church_Cathedral_Interior_1%2C_Oxford%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christ_Church_Cathedral_Interior_1,_Oxford,_UK_-_Diliff.jpg)

Choir and organ of Christ Church Cathedral

Long associated with [high church Anglicanism](/wiki/High_church "High church"),[[39]](#cite_note-39) Christ Church is unique in that it has both a cathedral choir and a college choir. The cathedral choir comprises twelve adults and sixteen boys. The adults are made up of lay clerks and choral scholars, or academical clerks. The choir was all male until 2019, when they welcomed alto Elizabeth Nurse, the first female clerk of Christ Church Cathedral Choir.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The boys, whose ages range from eight to thirteen, are chosen for their musical ability and attend [Christ Church Cathedral School](/wiki/Christ_Church_Cathedral_School "Christ Church Cathedral School"). Aside from the director, [Peter Holder](/wiki/Peter_Holder_(organist) "Peter Holder (organist)"), there is also a sub-organist and two organ scholars. The college choir, however, is always a student-run society, and sings Evensong once a week in term time. In vacations the services are sung by the Cathedral Singers of Christ Church – a choir drawn from semi-professional singers in and around Oxford. The cathedral also hosts visiting choirs from time to time during vacations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Christ_Church_Cathedral_Interior_2%2C_Oxford%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg/250px-Christ_Church_Cathedral_Interior_2%2C_Oxford%2C_UK_-_Diliff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christ_Church_Cathedral_Interior_2,_Oxford,_UK_-_Diliff.jpg)

Nave of Christ Church Cathedral looking to the altar

Throughout its history, the cathedral choir has attracted many distinguished composers and organists – from its first director, [John Taverner](/wiki/John_Taverner "John Taverner"), appointed by [Cardinal Wolsey](/wiki/Cardinal_Wolsey "Cardinal Wolsey") in 1526, to [William Walton](/wiki/William_Walton "William Walton") in the twentieth century. In recent years, the choir have commissioned recorded works by contemporary composers such as [John Tavener](/wiki/John_Tavener "John Tavener"), [William Mathias](/wiki/William_Mathias "William Mathias") and [Howard Goodall](/wiki/Howard_Goodall "Howard Goodall"), also patron of Christ Church Music Society.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The choir, which broadcasts regularly, have many recordings to their credit and were the subject of the [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4") television documentary *Howard Goodall's Great Dates* (2002). The documentary was nominated at the Montreux TV Festival in the arts programme category – and has since been seen internationally. The choir's collaboration with Goodall has also led to their singing his TV themes for *[Mr. Bean](/wiki/Mr._Bean "Mr. Bean")* and *[Vicar of Dibley](/wiki/Vicar_of_Dibley "Vicar of Dibley")*. They appeared in *Howard Goodall's Big Bangs*, broadcast in the United Kingdom on Channel 4 in March 2000. *Treasures of Christ Church* (2011) is an example of the choir's recording and debuted as the highest new entry in the UK Specialist Classical chart.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The disc featured on BBC Radio 3's *In Tune* on 26 September 2011 and on Radio 3's *Breakfast Show* on 27 September that year.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Picture Gallery

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christ_Church,_Oxford&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Picture Gallery")]

Main article: [Christ Church Picture Gallery](/wiki/Christ_Church_Picture_Gallery "Christ Church Picture Gallery")

Christ Church holds one of the most important private collections of drawings in the UK, including works by [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci"), [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael "Raphael") and [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo"). The collection is composed of approximately 300 paintings and 2,000 drawings, a rotated selection of which are available to the public for viewing in the purpose-built [Christ Church Picture Gallery](/wiki/Christ_Church_Picture_Gallery "Christ Church Picture Gallery"). Many of the works were bequeathed by a former member of the college, [General John Guise](/wiki/John_Guise_(British_Army_officer) "John Guise (British Army officer)") (1682/3-1765), enabling the creation of the first public art gallery in Britain.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-FT-44)

## Coat of arms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christ_Church,_Oxford&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Coat of arms")]

### College arms

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Christ_Church_Oxford_Coat_Of_Arms.jpg/250px-Christ_Church_Oxford_Coat_Of_Arms.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christ_Church_Oxford_Coat_Of_Arms.jpg)

College arms

The [college arms](/wiki/Thomas_Wolsey#Arms "Thomas Wolsey") are those of [Cardinal Wolsey](/wiki/Cardinal_Wolsey "Cardinal Wolsey") and were granted to him by the [College of Arms](/wiki/College_of_Arms "College of Arms") on 4 August 1525.[[45]](#cite_note-45) They are blazoned: *Sable, on a cross engrailed argent, between four leopards' faces azure a lion passant gules; on a chief or between two Cornish choughs proper a rose gules barbed vert and seeded or*. The lion refers to [Leo X](/wiki/Leo_X "Leo X") who created Wolsey a Cardinal.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) The arms are depicted beneath a red cardinal's [galero](/wiki/Galero "Galero") with fifteen tassels on either side, and sometimes in front of two crossed croziers.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Graces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christ_Church,_Oxford&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Graces")]

The college [preprandial](/wiki/Preprandial "Preprandial") grace reads:

| Latin | English |
| --- | --- |
| *Nōs miserī hominēs et egēnī, prō cibīs quōs nōbis ad corporis subsidium benignē es largītus, tibi, Deus omnipotēns, Pater cælestis, grātiās reverenter agimus; simul obsecrantēs, ut iīs sobriē, modestē atque grātē ūtāmur.* *Īnsuper petimus, ut cibum angelōrum, vērum panem cælestem, verbum Deī æternum, Dominum nostrum Iēsum Christum, nōbis impertiāris; utque illō mēns nostra pascātur et per carnem et sanguinem eius fovēāmur, alāmur, et corrōborēmur. Āmen.* [[48]](#cite_note-48) | "We unhappy and unworthy men do give thee most reverent thanks, Almighty God, our heavenly Father, for the victuals which thou hast bestowed on us for the sustenance of the body, at the same time beseeching thee that we may use them soberly, modestly and gratefully. And above all we beseech thee to impart to us the food of angels, the true bread of heaven, the eternal Word of God, Jesus Christ our Lord, so that the mind of each of us may feed on him and that through his flesh and blood we may be sustained, nourished and strengthened. Amen." |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/ChristChurchMeadow.JPG/250px-ChristChurchMeadow.JPG)](/wiki/File:ChristChurchMeadow.JPG)

Christ Church from the south- east across Christ Church Meadow

The first part of the grace is read by a scholar or exhibitioner before [formal hall](/wiki/Formal_hall "Formal hall") each evening, ending with the words *Per Iēsum Christum Dominum nostrum* ("Through Jesus Christ our Lord.") The remainder of the grace, replacing *Per Iēsum Christum* etc., is usually only read on special occasions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

There is also a long postprandial grace intended for use after meals, but this is rarely used. When High Table rises (by which time the Hall is largely empty), the senior member on High Table simply says *Benedictō benedīcātur* ("Let the Blessed One be blessed", or "Let a blessing be given by the Blessed One"), instead of the college postprandial grace.

## Student life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christ_Church,_Oxford&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Student life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Christ_Church%2C_College_Hall%2C_Oxford_2010.jpg/250px-Christ_Church%2C_College_Hall%2C_Oxford_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christ_Church,_College_Hall,_Oxford_2010.jpg)

Christ Church Great Hall

As well as rooms for accommodation, the buildings of Christ Church include the cathedral, one of the smallest in England, which also acts as the college chapel, a great hall, two libraries, two bars, and separate [common rooms](/wiki/Common_room_(university) "Common room (university)") for dons, graduates and undergraduates. There are also gardens and a neighbouring sports ground and boat-house.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Accommodation is usually provided for all undergraduates, and for some graduates, although some accommodation is off-site. Accommodation is generally spacious with most rooms equipped with sinks and fridges. Many undergraduate rooms comprise 'sets' of bedrooms and living areas. Members are generally expected to dine in hall, where there are two sittings every evening, one informal and one formal (where gowns must be worn and Latin grace is read). The college offers subsidies on the costs of accommodation and dinners for UK and ROI students from families with lower household incomes.[[49]](#cite_note-49) The [buttery](/wiki/Buttery_(shop) "Buttery (shop)") next to the Hall serves drinks around dinner time. There is also a college bar (known as the Undercroft), as well as a [Junior Common Room](/wiki/Junior_Common_Room "Junior Common Room") (JCR) and a Graduate Common Room (GCR), equivalent to the [Middle Common Room](/wiki/Common_Room_(university) "Common Room (university)") (MCR) in other colleges.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

There is a college lending library that supplements the university libraries (many of which are non-lending). Law students have the additional facility of the Burn Law Library, named for [Edward Burn](/wiki/Edward_Burn_(legal_scholar) "Edward Burn (legal scholar)").[[50]](#cite_note-50) Most undergraduate tutorials are carried out in the college, though for some specialist subjects undergraduates may be sent to tutors in other colleges.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Croquet](/wiki/Croquet "Croquet") is played in the Masters' Garden in the summer. The sports ground is mainly used for netball, cricket, tennis, rugby and football and includes [Christ Church cricket ground](/wiki/Christ_Church_Ground "Christ Church Ground"). In recent years the Christ Church Netball Club, which competes on the inter-college level in both mixed and women's matches, has become known as a popular and inclusive sport. Rowing and [punting](/wiki/Punt_(boat) "Punt (boat)") is carried out by the boat-house across [Christ Church Meadow](/wiki/Christ_Church_Meadow,_Oxford "Christ Church Meadow, Oxford") – the [Christ Church Boat Club](/wiki/Christ_Church_Boat_Club "Christ Church Boat Club") is traditionally strong at rowing, having been Head of the River more than all other colleges except [Oriel College](/wiki/Oriel_College "Oriel College"). The college also owns its own punts which may be borrowed by students or dons.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The college [beagle](/wiki/Beagling "Beagling") pack (Christ Church and Farley Hill Beagles), which was formerly one of several undergraduate packs in Oxford, is no longer formally connected with the college or the university but continues to be staffed and followed by some Oxford undergraduates.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Christ Church references

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christ_Church,_Oxford&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Christ Church references")]

> "Midnight has come and the great Christ Church bell
>
> And many a lesser bell sound through the room;
>
> And it is All Souls' Night..."

— [W B Yeats](/wiki/W_B_Yeats "W B Yeats"), *All Souls' Night*, Oxford (1920)

> "The wind had dropped. There was even a glimpse of the moon riding behind the clouds. And now, a solemn and plangent token of Oxford's perpetuity, the first stroke of Great Tom sounded."

— [Max Beerbohm](/wiki/Max_Beerbohm "Max Beerbohm"), Chapter 21, *[Zuleika Dobson](/wiki/Zuleika_Dobson "Zuleika Dobson")* (1922)

> "I must say my thoughts wandered, but I kept turning the pages and watching the light fade, which in Peckwater, my dear, is quite an experience – as darkness falls the stone seems positively to decay under one's eyes. I was reminded of some of those leprous façades in the *vieux port* at Marseille, until suddenly I was disturbed by such a bawling and caterwauling as you never heard, and there, down in the little piazza, I saw a mob of about twenty terrible young men, and do you know what they were chanting *We want Blanche. We want Blanche!* in a kind of litany."

— [Evelyn Waugh](/wiki/Evelyn_Waugh "Evelyn Waugh"), *[Brideshead Revisited](/wiki/Brideshead_Revisited "Brideshead Revisited")* (1945)

> "Those twins / Of learning that he [Wolsey] raised in you,
>
> Ipswich and Oxford! one of which fell with him,   
> Unwilling to outlive the good that did it;   
> The other, though unfinish'd, yet so famous,   
> So excellent in art, and still so rising,
>
> That Christendom shall ever speak his virtue."

— [William Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare"), [*Henry VIII*](/wiki/Henry_VIII_(play) "Henry VIII (play)") (1613)

> "By way of light entertainment, I should tell the Committee that it is well known that a match between an archer and a golfer can be fairly close. I spent many a happy evening in the centre of [Peckwater Quadrangle](/wiki/Peckwater_Quadrangle "Peckwater Quadrangle") at Christ Church, with a bow and arrow, trying to put an arrow over the Kilcannon building into the Mercury Pond in [Tom Quad](/wiki/Tom_Quad "Tom Quad"). On occasion, the golfer would win and, on occasion, I would win. Unfortunately, that had to stop when I put an arrow through the bowler hat of the head porter. Luckily, he was unhurt and bore me no ill will. From that time on he always sent me a Christmas card which was signed 'To Robin Hood from the Ancient Briton'"

— Lord Crawshaw, [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), *[Hansard](/wiki/Hansard "Hansard")* (Tuesday 8 July 1997)

> "There is one oddity; Rudge. Determined to try for Oxford, Christ Church of all places! Might get into [Loughborough](/wiki/Loughborough_University "Loughborough University"), in a bad year."

— [Alan Bennett](/wiki/Alan_Bennett "Alan Bennett"), *[The History Boys](/wiki/The_History_Boys "The History Boys")* (2004)

> " And once, in winter, on the causeway chill
>
> Where home through flooded fields foot-travellers go,   
> Have I not pass'd thee on the wooden bridge,   
> Wrapt in thy cloak and battling with the snow,   
> And thou has climb'd the hill,   
> And gain'd the white brow of the Cumner range;   
> Turn'd once to watch, while thick the snowflakes fall,   
> The line of festal light in Christ-Church hall—
>
> Then sought thy straw in some sequester'd grange. "

— [Matthew Arnold](/wiki/Matthew_Arnold "Matthew Arnold"), *[The Scholar Gypsy](/wiki/The_Scholar_Gypsy "The Scholar Gypsy")* (1853)

Also included in: [Vaughan Williams](/wiki/Vaughan_Williams "Vaughan Williams")'s *[An Oxford Elegy](/wiki/An_Oxford_Elegy "An Oxford Elegy")* (1947–49) and [Hilary Mantel](/wiki/Hilary_Mantel "Hilary Mantel")'s [Wolf Hall](/wiki/Wolf_Hall "Wolf Hall") trilogy (referred to by its previous name, Cardinal College).

Notable former students of the college have included politicians, scientists, philosophers, entertainers and academics. Thirteen [British prime ministers](/wiki/List_of_Prime_Ministers_of_the_United_Kingdom_by_education "List of Prime Ministers of the United Kingdom by education") have studied at the college including, [Anthony Eden](/wiki/Anthony_Eden "Anthony Eden") (Prime Minister 1955–1957), [William Ewart Gladstone](/wiki/William_Ewart_Gladstone "William Ewart Gladstone") (1828–1831), [Sir Robert Peel](/wiki/Robert_Peel "Robert Peel") (1841–1846) and [Archibald Primrose](/wiki/Archibald_Primrose,_5th_Earl_of_Rosebery "Archibald Primrose, 5th Earl of Rosebery") (1894–1895). Other former students include [Charles Abbot](/wiki/Charles_Abbot,_1st_Baron_Colchester "Charles Abbot, 1st Baron Colchester") ([Speaker](/wiki/Speaker_(politics) "Speaker (politics)") of the [House of Commons](/wiki/United_Kingdom_House_of_Commons "United Kingdom House of Commons") 1802–1817), [Frederick Curzon](/wiki/Frederick_Curzon,_7th_Earl_Howe "Frederick Curzon, 7th Earl Howe") ([Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") statesman 1951–), [Nicholas Lyell](/wiki/Nicholas_Lyell,_Baron_Lyell_of_Markyate "Nicholas Lyell, Baron Lyell of Markyate") ([Attorney General](/wiki/Attorney_General_for_England_and_Wales "Attorney General for England and Wales") 1992–1997), [Nigel Lawson](/wiki/Nigel_Lawson "Nigel Lawson") ([Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer") 1983–1989), [Quintin Hogg](/wiki/Quintin_Hogg,_Baron_Hailsham_of_St_Marylebone "Quintin Hogg, Baron Hailsham of St Marylebone") ([Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor") 1979–1987) and [William Murray](/wiki/William_Murray,_1st_Earl_of_Mansfield "William Murray, 1st Earl of Mansfield") ([Lord Chief Justice](/wiki/Lord_Chief_Justice_of_England "Lord Chief Justice of England") 1756–1788 and [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer") 1757). From outside the UK, politicians from Canada ([Ted Jolliffe](/wiki/Ted_Jolliffe "Ted Jolliffe")), Pakistan ([Zulfikar Ali Bhutto](/wiki/Zulfikar_Ali_Bhutto "Zulfikar Ali Bhutto")), Sri Lanka ([S. W. R. D. Bandaranaike](/wiki/S._W._R._D._Bandaranaike "S. W. R. D. Bandaranaike")), Pakistan ([Bilawal Bhutto Zardari](/wiki/Bilawal_Bhutto_Zardari "Bilawal Bhutto Zardari")) and the United States ([Charles Cotesworth Pinckney](/wiki/Charles_Cotesworth_Pinckney "Charles Cotesworth Pinckney")) have attended the college.

Prominent philosophers including [John Locke](/wiki/John_Locke "John Locke"), [John Rawls](/wiki/John_Rawls "John Rawls"), [A. J. Ayer](/wiki/A._J._Ayer "A. J. Ayer"), [Gilbert Ryle](/wiki/Gilbert_Ryle "Gilbert Ryle"), [Michael Dummett](/wiki/Michael_Dummett "Michael Dummett"), [John Searle](/wiki/John_Searle "John Searle") and [Daniel Dennett](/wiki/Daniel_Dennett "Daniel Dennett") studied at Christ Church.

There are numerous former students in the fields of academia and theology, including [George Kitchin](/wiki/George_Kitchin "George Kitchin") (the first [Chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_(education) "Chancellor (education)") of the [University of Durham](/wiki/University_of_Durham "University of Durham") 1908–1912 and [Dean of Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Dean_of_Durham_Cathedral "Dean of Durham Cathedral") 1894–1912), [John Charles Ryle](/wiki/John_Charles_Ryle "John Charles Ryle") (first [Bishop of Liverpool](/wiki/Bishop_of_Liverpool "Bishop of Liverpool") 1880–1900), [John Wesley](/wiki/John_Wesley "John Wesley") (leader of the [Methodist movement](/wiki/Methodist_movement "Methodist movement")), [Rowan Williams](/wiki/Rowan_Williams "Rowan Williams") ([Archbishop of Canterbury](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Canterbury "Archbishop of Canterbury") 2002–2012), [Richard William Jelf](/wiki/Richard_William_Jelf "Richard William Jelf") (Principal of [King's College London](/wiki/King%27s_College_London "King's College London") 1843–1868), [Ronald Montagu Burrows](/wiki/Ronald_Montagu_Burrows "Ronald Montagu Burrows") (Principal of [King's College London](/wiki/King%27s_College_London "King's College London") 1913–1920) and [William Stubbs](/wiki/William_Stubbs "William Stubbs") (Bishop of Oxford 1889–1901 and historian).

Two Olympic [rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)") gold medallists studied at the college: [Jonny Searle](/wiki/Jonny_Searle "Jonny Searle") and Spanish Civil War volunteer [Lewis Clive](/wiki/Lewis_Clive "Lewis Clive").[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

In the sciences, [polymath](/wiki/Polymath "Polymath") and [natural philosopher](/wiki/Natural_philosopher "Natural philosopher") [Robert Hooke](/wiki/Robert_Hooke "Robert Hooke"), [developmental biologist](/wiki/Developmental_biologist "Developmental biologist") [John B. Gurdon](/wiki/John_B._Gurdon "John B. Gurdon") (co-winner of the 2012 [Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine")), physician [Sir Archibald Edward Garrod](/wiki/Archibald_Garrod "Archibald Garrod"), the Father of Modern Medicine [Sir William Osler](/wiki/Sir_William_Osler "Sir William Osler"), biochemist [Kenneth Callow](/wiki/Kenneth_Callow "Kenneth Callow"), radio astronomer [Sir Martin Ryle](/wiki/Martin_Ryle "Martin Ryle"), psychologist [Edward de Bono](/wiki/Edward_de_Bono "Edward de Bono") and epidemiologist [Sir Richard Doll](/wiki/Richard_Doll "Richard Doll") are all associated with the college. [Albert Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein") was a learned research fellow.

In other fields, [Cameron](/wiki/Cameron_Winklevoss "Cameron Winklevoss") and [Tyler Winklevoss](/wiki/Tyler_Winklevoss "Tyler Winklevoss"), twins associated with the founding of [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook"), King [Edward VII](/wiki/Edward_VII_of_the_United_Kingdom "Edward VII of the United Kingdom") (1841–1910), [King of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom") and [Emperor of India](/wiki/Emperor_of_India "Emperor of India"), [King William II](/wiki/William_II_of_the_Netherlands "William II of the Netherlands") of the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), [Prince Paul of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Prince_Paul_of_Yugoslavia "Prince Paul of Yugoslavia"), entrepreneur and founder of Pennsylvania [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn"), broadcaster [David Dimbleby](/wiki/David_Dimbleby "David Dimbleby"), MP [Louise Mensch](/wiki/Louise_Mensch "Louise Mensch"), BBC composer [Howard Goodall](/wiki/Howard_Goodall "Howard Goodall"), actor [Riz Ahmed](/wiki/Riz_Ahmed "Riz Ahmed"), the writer [Lewis Carroll](/wiki/Lewis_Carroll "Lewis Carroll"), poet [W. H. Auden](/wiki/W._H._Auden "W. H. Auden"), and the former officer of arms [Hubert Chesshyre](/wiki/Hubert_Chesshyre "Hubert Chesshyre") are other notable students to have previously studied at Christ Church.
